raw_name: Soil organic matter (organic carbon in topsoil, up to 30cm depth)
quote: Soil organic matter — Organic carbon content in the topsoil layer, up to 30cm depth, contained in (decomposed) plant and animal residues, tissues and cells (Nachtergaele et al., 2012). 'Soil organic matter' is an indicator of soil fertility and, thus, ecosystem productivity. Regions with low soil organic carbon need CSA practices that alleviate nutrient depletion such as measures to stop erosion to build up soil carbon through organic fertilizers and integrated practices such as agroforestry (Lal et al., 2011).

Slopes of 3-15% are optimal for contour-based agroforestry, while slopes exceeding 30% present severe operational limits for mechanized cultivation.
Agroforestry systems in Sub-Saharan Africa are highly successful in areas receiving more than 800 mm of annual rainfall, where moisture is sufficient to support both trees and crops.
Typical establishment costs for crop-associated agroforestry range between 50 and 150 USD per hectare depending on seedling sourcing and labor inputs.
Runoff farming is most suited to gentle slopes of up to 5%, while macro-catchment systems can tolerate slopes up to 60%.
Water harvesting is primarily applied in semi-arid and arid regions with annual rainfall between 200 mm and 750 mm.
Initial construction and labor costs for micro-catchment water harvesting systems are estimated at 150 to 300 USD per hectare.

The technical framework establishes four essential functions for NBS in agriculture: 1) Sustainable practices — production; 2) Green Infrastructure; 3) Amelioration; and 4) Conservation — focusing on biodiversity and connectivity.

Secure land tenure is frequently identified as a critical factor for the long-term success of agroforestry initiatives, as farmers are more likely to invest in tree planting and soil health when they have guaranteed rights to their land.
Financing remains a significant hurdle. Transitioning requires bridging the climate finance gap by developing bankable projects to attract investment.
Restoring and protecting forests and floodplains in Sub-Saharan Africa shows high cost-effectiveness, with typical restoration costs averaging 80 to 200 USD per hectare depending on the subpractice.
